title-group.article-title: CsrA Regulation via Binding to the Base-pairing Small RNA Spot 42

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Microbiology and Cell Science, Institute of Food and Agricultural Sciences, University of Florida, Gainesville, Florida 32611, USA
2Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, Center for RNA Molecular Biology, The Pennsylvania State University, University Park, Pennsylvania 16802, USA

fn.p: Y.L. conceived and designed the study, conducted experiments, collected and analyzed data, and wrote the manuscript. H.Y. conducted footprint assays, RNase E cleavage experiments and coupled transcription-translation assays. A.P. conducted β-galactosidase experiments and EMSA for RNA-RNA interactions. C.P. gave useful critiques and contributed to editing the original draft. P.B. acquired funding, supervised the work, and edited the manuscript. T.R. acquired funding, conceived the study, supervised the work and contributed to writing the manuscript.

abstract.title: Summary
abstract.p: The Csr system and base-pairing small RNAs represent two predominant modes of bacterial posttranscriptional regulation, which globally influence gene expression. Binding of CsrA protein to the 5’ UTR or initial mRNA coding sequences can affect translation, RNA stability, and/or transcript elongation. Base-pairing sRNAs also regulate these processes, often requiring assistance from the RNA chaperone Hfq. Transcriptomics studies in Escherichia coli have identified many new CsrA targets, including Spot 42 and other base-pairing sRNAs. Spot 42 synthesis is repressed by cAMP-CRP, induced by the presence of glucose, and Spot 42 posttranscriptionally represses operons that facilitate metabolism of nonpreferred carbon sources. CsrA activity is also increased by glucose via effects on CsrA sRNA antagonists, CsrB/C. Here, we elucidate a mechanism wherein CsrA binds to and protects Spot 42 sRNA from RNase E-mediated cleavage. This protection leads to enhanced repression of srlA by Spot 42, a gene required for sorbitol uptake. A second, independent mechanism by which CsrA represses srlA is by binding to and inhibiting translation of srlM mRNA, encoding a transcriptional activator of srlA. Our findings demonstrate a novel means of regulation, by CsrA binding to an sRNA, and indicate that such interactions can help to shape complex bacterial regulatory circuitry.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: To adapt to changing environmental conditions, bacteria have evolved complex genetic networks that allow them to integrate incoming signals and cues and use this information to coordinate their metabolism and physiology. Two kinds of posttranscriptional regulatory systems that globally impact genetic circuitry of Gammaproteobacteria are Csr (carbon storage regulator) systems and small RNA (sRNA) based systems. The latter often depend on Hfq protein to mediate base-pairing interactions with mRNA transcripts (Pourciau et al., 2020, Hör et al., 2020).

In Escherichia coli and Salmonella typhimurium, the Csr system consists of four main components: CsrA, two noncoding small RNAs, CsrB and CsrC, and the GGDEF-EAL domain protein, CsrD. CsrA is the primary component of the Csr system (Pourciau et al., 2020). It is an RNA binding protein that targets GGA-containing sequence elements of mRNAs (Dubey et al., 2005, Potts et al., 2017) involved in processes such as central carbon metabolism (Sabnis et al., 1995, Morin et al., 2016, Revelles et al., 2013), quorum sensing (Yakhnin et al., 2011), stress response processes (Edwards et al., 2011, Pannuri et al., 2016, Yakhnin et al., 2017), motility (Wei et al., 2001), biofilm formation (Jackson et al., 2002, Wang et al., 2005, Pannuri et al., 2012), iron storage (Pourciau et al., 2019), and virulence pathways of pathogens (Vakulskas et al., 2015). CsrA is sequestered away from mRNA by CsrB and CsrC sRNAs, which contain multiple high affinity CsrA-binding GGA sequences (Babitzke & Romeo, 2007). CsrB/C expression responds to numerous cues and signals, including preferred carbon sources (Leng et al., 2016, Vakulskas et al., 2016, Romeo & Babitzke, 2018), metabolic products such as formate and acetate (Chavez et al., 2010, Zere et al., 2015), amino acid limitation (Edwards et al., 2011) and extracytoplasmic stress (Yakhnin et al., 2017). The turnover of CsrB/C is RNase E-mediated and triggered by glucose via interaction of unphosphorylated EIIAglc with the CsrD EAL domain (Leng et al., 2016, Vakulskas et al., 2016). Thus, CsrA activity can be negatively regulated in response to stresses, the absence of a preferred carbon source, and the accumulation of metabolic end products.

The global role of the Csr system in posttranscriptional gene regulation has been established through studies beginning almost 3 decades ago (Romeo & Gong, 1993, Romeo et al., 1993). Recently, integrated transcriptomics analyses (CLIP-seq, RNA decay, ribosome profiling) have revealed global effects of CsrA on gene expression, mediated via regulatory interactions with hundreds of mRNAs including dozens encoding transcription factors and binding interactions with several sRNAs (Potts et al., 2017). In addition to CsrB/C, 6 base-pairing sRNAs (Spot 42, FnrS, GcvB, CyaR, GadY and MicL) were crosslinked to CsrA in CLIP-seq analyses. No function has been attributed to the interactions with the base-pairing sRNAs. The observation that CsrA binds to base-pairing sRNAs suggests possible new regulatory roles, mechanisms and circuitry for CsrA, which we examine here for Spot 42.

Small RNAs (sRNAs) modulate diverse physiological circuits and impact cell viability and competitiveness (Gottesman & Storz, 2011, Nitzan et al., 2017, Carrier et al., 2018). Typically ~50–300 nucleotides in length, sRNAs are highly structured and occasionally may be translated into proteins. In E. coli and Salmonella, more than 100 different sRNAs have been identified, many of which are conserved in related species (Adams & Storz, 2020). sRNAs are typically expressed in response to stress conditions, such as glucose or iron starvation (Chareyre & Mandin, 2018), oxidative stress (Altuvia et al., 1997), unfavorable temperature (Sledjeski et al., 1996) or other stressors (Gottesman & Storz, 2011, Storz et al., 2011). sRNAs fall into two distinct classes: cis-acting and trans-acting (Waters & Storz, 2009). Cis-acting sRNAs are transcribed from the same region of DNA, but on the opposite strand with respect to their mRNA targets. With perfect complementarity, they do not require additional factors to anneal with mRNA. In contrast, trans-acting sRNAs are transcribed from different genomic loci than their mRNA targets and often require the assistance of a chaperone protein, such as Hfq, for efficient pairing (Vogel & Luisi, 2011, Updegrove et al., 2016, Kavita et al., 2018). A single trans-acting sRNA may regulate multiple mRNA targets through interactions at more than one seed region. sRNA:mRNA pairing can positively or negatively regulate translation and mRNA stability (Fröhlich & Vogel, 2009, Caron et al., 2010, De Lay et al., 2013, Desnoyers et al., 2013).

Hfq is often the limiting factor in base-pairing interactions (Wagner, 2013, Hussein & Lim, 2011). Estimates of its levels in E. coli range from 400 (Carmichael et al., 1975) to 10,000 hexamers per cell (Kajitani et al., 1994) or approximately 0.8 to 20 μM. Hfq forms a homohexameric ring-like structure containing four potential sites for interaction with nucleic acids (Sauer, 2013). This arrangement allows Hfq to bind to two RNA molecules simultaneously and accelerate sRNA-mRNA duplex formation (Vogel & Luisi, 2011, Santiago-Frangos & Woodson, 2018). The proximal face of Hfq preferentially interacts with single-stranded A/U-rich sequences near the intrinsic terminator hairpin of sRNAs, while the distal face interacts with (ARN)x motifs, often found in the 5’UTR of mRNAs (Schu et al., 2015, Peng et al., 2014). Hfq binding can remodel RNA secondary structures and stabilize sRNA-mRNA duplexes (Lease & Woodson, 2004, Henderson et al., 2013, Wagner, 2013). CLIP-seq analysis of Hfq-binding partners in E. coli and Salmonella revealed up to 100 sRNAs that may interact with more than 25% of cellular mRNAs via Hfq-dependent pairing (Holmqvist et al., 2016, Holmqvist et al., 2018). Other bacterial proteins such as ProQ and RapZ can also regulate sRNA:mRNA base-pairing interactions (Holmqvist & Vogel, 2018, Khan et al., 2020).

Spot 42 is a 109-nucleotide Hfq-dependent and Hfq-coimmunopreciptated sRNA transcribed from the spf (spot forty-two) gene and present at up to 200 copies per cell or ~400 nM (Sahagan & Dahlberg, 1979; Møller et al., 2002a). Its transcription is repressed by the cAMP-CRP complex (Joyce & Grindley, 1982). The spf gene is highly conserved in Enterobacteriaceae (Rice & Dahlberg, 1982, Bækkedal & Haugen, 2015). The Spot 42 structure contains two hairpins separated by a ~10 nt single-stranded region and an intrinsic terminator located immediately downstream of the second hairpin (Møller et al., 2002b, Bækkedal & Haugen, 2015). Spot 42 base-pairs with cognate mRNAs via three conserved seed regions (Beisel & Storz, 2011a, Beisel & Storz, 2011b, Bækkedal & Haugen, 2015). Many of the genes repressed by Spot 42 encode transporters or catabolic enzymes for non-preferred carbon sources and are transcriptionally activated by cAMP-CRP (Beisel & Storz, 2011b). Thus, Spot 42 participates in a multioutput coherent feedforward loop that reinforces cAMP-CRP regulation (Beisel & Storz, 2011b). Additional mRNA targets of sRNAs, including Spot 42, have been identified by RIL-seq or CLASH approaches (Melamed et al., 2016, Melamed et al., 2020, Iosub et al., 2020).

Spot 42 negatively regulates srlA, the first gene of the srl locus (Beisel & Storz, 2011b) (Fig. 1). D-sorbitol is a naturally occurring hexitol that enters E. coli via a phosphotransferase system, encoded at the srlAEBDM-srlRQ locus (Yamada & Saier, 1987b, Yamada & Saier, 1987a, Mayer & Boos, 2005). Expression of srlA involves the transcriptional activator SrlM, the transcriptional repressor SrlR, and cAMP-CRP (Yamada & Saier, 1988) (Fig. 1). Transcriptomics analyses suggested that CsrA negatively regulates srlA mRNA levels and srlM mRNA stability (Potts et al., 2017). Thus, CsrA might regulate srlA expression (i) indirectly via its interaction with Spot 42, (ii) through indirect effects via srlM or (iii) via direct effects on srlA translation or stability. We show that CsrA regulation of srlA occurs via mechanisms (i) and (ii), but not (iii), generating a coherent negative feed-forward circuit with Spot 42.

sec.title: CsrA Binds to Spot 42 RNA with High Affinity and Specificity
sec.p: To begin to investigate the regulatory connections between CsrA and base-pairing sRNAs, we examined the interaction of CsrA with Spot 42 RNA. Spot 42 is an Hfq-dependent sRNA that regulates more than a dozen genes, many of which are involved in carbon metabolism (Beisel & Storz, 2011b, Melamed et al., 2016). Electrophoretic mobility gel shift assays (EMSAs) were performed to assess CsrA binding to 5’-end-radiolabeled Spot 42 RNA probes, which were synthesized in vitro using pSpot42 (Table S1) as the template DNA. Spot 42 RNA was shifted by increasing concentrations of CsrA. A complete shift was observed at 160 nM. A nonlinear least-square analysis of these data yielded an apparent KD (equilibrium dissociation constant) of 66 nM (Fig. 2A). This value is within the range observed previously for CsrA-regulated mRNAs (Dubey et al., 2005, Mercante et al., 2009), and is much lower than the intracellular concentration of CsrA, ~20 μM (Mercante et al., 2006, Gudapaty et al., 2001). Competition assays performed with specific (Spot 42) and nonspecific (phoB) unlabeled competitor transcripts confirmed that CsrA binding to Spot 42 is specific (Fig. 2B). Because Spot 42 functions in an Hfq-dependent manner (Møller et al., 2002a, Møller et al., 2002b, Zhang et al., 2003), EMSAs were performed to examine the Spot 42-Hfq binding interaction. Hfq shifted labeled Spot 42 RNA with an apparent KD of 3.7 nM (Fig. 2C), confirming a high affinity interaction, consistent with previous findings (Møller et al., 2002a). Spot 42 exhibited a supershift at higher Hfq concentrations, suggesting the formation of a complex containing two Hfq molecules bound to an sRNA, which has been observed in previous studies of Hfq interaction (Lease & Woodson, 2004). The basis of the Hfq-Spot 42 supershift was not further investigated.

sec.title: Hfq and CsrA Bind to Spot 42 Individually and Together
sec.p: Examination of CLIP-seq data suggested that CsrA and Hfq bind to distinct segments of Spot 42 from E. coli and Salmonella, respectively (Potts et al., 2017, Holmqvist et al., 2016) (Fig. 3A). It is noteworthy that spf genes in E. coli and in Salmonella are nearly identical, with only two nucleotide differences (nt 90 is A in E. coli, and is T followed by an additional T in Salmonella) and their hfq genes are more than 90% identical at the nucleotide and amino acid levels, with all amino acid deviations located in the Hfq C-terminal region (Sittka et al., 2007). To investigate simultaneous CsrA and Hfq interaction with Spot 42 RNA, we performed an EMSA with various concentrations of CsrA and/or Hfq. CsrA and/or Hfq were present in the reactions prior to the addition of Spot 42. Reactions were assembled on ice and then incubated at 37°C for 30 min. Spot 42 RNA shifted with increasing concentrations of CsrA and Hfq added individually (Fig. 2D, lanes 1–5). At lower concentrations of CsrA (75 nM) together with 2 nM Hfq, the majority of Spot 42 remained unbound, while two higher shifted complexes formed Spot 42-Hfq and Spot 42-Hfq-CsrA (Fig. 2D, lane 6). At higher concentrations of CsrA (150 nM), the intensity of the upper shifts appeared to increase slightly (Fig. 2D, lane 7). The presence of 20 nM Hfq greatly increased the formation of Hfq-bound complexes, Spot 42-Hfq and Spot 42-Hfq-CsrA, leaving almost no unbound Spot 42 (Fig. 2D, lane 8). A further increase in CsrA to 150 nM (Fig. 2D, lane 9) caused an increase in the upper complex, Spot 42-Hfq-CsrA, at the expense of the Spot 42-Hfq complex. These results show that CsrA and Hfq can bind to Spot 42 RNA independently of each other and together.

sec.title: Mutation of the predicted CsrA Binding Motif in Spot 42 Weakens Its Binding to CsrA
sec.p: The consensus sequence for high-affinity binding by E. coli CsrA was previously identified by a combinatorial library using systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX). The SELEX-derived CsrA binding site consensus sequence is ruACArGGAuG, with the GGA motif located within the single-stranded loops of RNA hairpins, which facilitated binding (Dubey et al., 2005). The spf sequence contains one GGA motif, which coincides with the CsrA CLIP-seq peak identified previously (Potts et al., 2017) (Fig. 3A). Note that while CsrA contains two different RNA-binding surfaces, it is capable of binding tightly to RNAs containing only one GGA motif (Mercante et al., 2009; Baker et al., 2007) This segment of Spot 42 is involved in direct base-pairing interactions with several validated-mRNA targets (Beisel & Storz, 2011b, Bækkedal & Haugen, 2015). To examine the involvement of this region in the Spot 42 interaction with CsrA, point mutations (GGA to ACA) were introduced into the spf gene, which was then expressed in trans from an IPTG inducible promoter. The resulting plasmid was named pSpot42_ACA. RNA folding predictions using the Mfold algorithm (Zuker, 2003) indicated that this alteration should not cause unintended structural rearrangement of Spot 42. A mutant Spot 42 RNA was generated using pSpot42_ACA as the template DNA for in vitro transcription, and the resulting RNA product was examined for CsrA binding using EMSA. The mutant Spot 42 RNA exhibited a ~2.2-fold reduction in CsrA binding affinity, with an apparent KD of ~150 nM (Fig. 3B) vs. the wild type (WT) Spot 42 (Fig. 2A, KD=66 nM). Previous analysis of a high-affinity CsrA-binding RNA ligand showed that substitutions of either G residue in the conserved GGA motif led to a 20-fold reduction in binding affinity (Dubey et al., 2005), much greater than observed here. While we cannot account for the weaker effects of the Spot 42 GGA to ACA substitution on CsrA binding, our results indicate that the GGA sequence plays a role in promoting CsrA binding.

sec.title: CsrA Up-Regulates Spot 42 RNA Levels
sec.p: To assess a possible role for CsrA in regulating spf expression, we performed Northern blotting with RNA isolated from WT vs. a csrA gene disruption mutant (csrA::gm), which has been examined extensively in previous studies (Vakulskas et al., 2016, Potts et al., 2018, Pourciau et al., 2019). RNA was isolated from cells grown in Kornberg (KB) medium containing 0.5% glucose, which was predicted to permit robust CsrA activity and Spot 42 expression, as well as in KB with 0.25% glycerol to decrease catabolite repression. Spot 42 expression from cells at mid-exponential phase (OD600=0.4) was determined by hybridization to a full-length riboprobe. Spot 42 RNA steady-state levels were significantly increased by CsrA in both glucose (Fig. 4A, inset) and glycerol (Fig. 4B, inset).

sec.title: CsrA Stabilizes Spot 42 in vivo
sec.p: To better understand how CsrA may increase Spot 42 cellular levels, we measured the stability of this sRNA in the WT and csrA::gm strains following the addition of rifampicin to exponentially growing cultures. The half-life of Spot 42 for strains grown in glucose was >16 min for the WT strain and ~4 min in the csrA::gm mutant (Fig. 4A). No prominent Spot 42 decay intermediates were detected in these experiments (data not shown). By growing in media containing 0.25% glycerol, which leads to reduced expression of spf (Møller et al., 2002b), a similar decay pattern was observed (Fig. 4B). Furthermore, Spot 42 exhibited biphasic decay, which was most evident in the csrA::gm strain. This biphasic pattern was not unexpected for a base-pairing sRNA in the presence of rifampicin. Coupled degradation of sRNA upon pairing with mRNA targets has been proposed to explain the initial rapid phase of sRNA decay, followed by a slower decay rate after mRNA targets have been essentially eliminated (Massé et al., 2003). Rifampicin treatment halts the transcription of both RNAs, quickly limiting the pool of target mRNAs, which have relatively short half-lives. Consequently, small RNAs often become stable in the presence of rifampicin once their mRNA targets have been expended.

To address this complication, decay curves of Spot 42 RNA were also measured with spf expressed from an arabinose inducible promoter (PBAD) (Fig. S1), which allows for transcriptional control of Spot 42 without blocking mRNA synthesis. Arabinose was added to exponentially growing cultures (OD600=0.4) to induce Spot 42 expression. After 15 min of induction, arabinose was removed by centrifugation and cells were resuspended in glucose-containing medium to stop Spot 42 synthesis. The half-life of Spot 42 RNA determined by this approach was 6 min in the WT strain and 3 min in the csrA::gm mutant (Fig. 4C). The RNA decay rates were derived from the rapid phases of decay, which was maintained for 4 to 8 min (Fig. 4C). Together, these decay studies conclusively show that CsrA stabilizes Spot 42 RNA in vivo.

sec.title: CsrA protects Spot 42 from RNase E-mediated decay in vivo
sec.p: RNase E is an essential endoribonuclease in E. coli that is responsible for bulk RNA turnover. A recent study generated a genome-wide in vivo RNase E cleavage map in Salmonella typhimurium, a close relative of E. coli (Chao et al., 2017). A minimal RNase E consensus motif was determined by aligning >22,000 cleavage sites, mostly occurring at AU-rich sequences with a uridine at the +2-position downstream of the cleavage site. In these analyses, one RNase E cleavage site was identified as the adenine at position 57 of Spot 42, which overlaps the sole GGA motif (Fig. 3A). Thus, we hypothesized that CsrA binding to the GGA sequence might stabilize Spot 42 RNA by protecting it from RNase E cleavage. To determine whether turnover of Spot 42 RNA is RNase E-dependent in E. coli, Spot 42 half-lives were measured in a temperature-sensitive rne-1 mutant in the presence or absence of CsrA using Northern blotting. The rne-1 mutant protein is active at 30°C but becomes inactive at 44°C (Suzuki et al., 2006, Babitzke & Kushner, 1991). Unfortunately, Spot 42 in the WT background becomes unstable at 44°C (data not shown). As a result, the non-permissive temperature utilized in this study was 40°C, a temperature at which Spot 42 is more stable in the WT strain (Fig. 4D and S2B). Inactivation of the mutant RNase E protein at 40°C was confirmed by measuring the decay of its well-studied substrate, rpsT mRNA (Mackie, 2013a) (Fig. S2A). Thus, RNA was collected from exponentially growing cultures following a temperature shift from 30°C to 40°C and with rifampicin addition (Fig. 4D and S2B). Spot 42 RNA became stable in rne-1 mutant strains at 40°C as well as in the rne-1 csrA::gm double mutant at 40°C, indicating that loss of CsrA activity did not destabilize Spot 42 when RNase E was inactive. These results confirmed that Spot 42 RNA decay is RNase E dependent and suggested a mechanism for CsrA-mediated protection from RNase E cleavage.

sec.title: CsrA and Hfq protect Spot 42 from RNase E-mediated cleavage in vitro
sec.p: To directly determine whether CsrA binding protects Spot 42 RNA from RNase E cleavage, in vitro cleavage assays were performed in the presence or absence of CsrA. As shown in Figure 5, RNase E caused cleavage at the previously reported residue (A57, named as site 1 hereafter) that overlaps the GGA sequence, as well as a second nearby site, A51 (named as site 2 hereafter) (Fig. 5, lane 2). Note that both sites possessed the minimal elements predicted for RNase E cleavage, (A residue, 2 nt upstream from U). In reactions containing 3 μM CsrA in addition to RNase E (Fig. 5, lane 4), the intensity of cleavage at both sites was decreased by ~70% as compared to the reactions without CsrA. Thus, CsrA directly protects the single-stranded region containing the GGA motif (Fig. 3A, nt 51–57) from RNase E cleavage.

To determine whether decreased binding affinity of CsrA to the Spot 42 GGA to ACA mutant sRNA (Fig. 2, 3) alters RNase E access to the RNA, a mutant Spot 42 RNA containing the GGA to ACA substitution was similarly examined for cleavage. In the absence of CsrA, the Spot 42 mutant RNA showed a ~70% reduction in intensity of RNase E cleavage at site 1 as compared to the WT Spot 42, indicating that the GGA sequence promotes RNase E cleavage (Fig. 5, lanes 2, 6). The GGA to ACA substitution had little or no effect on cleavage at site 2, as <10% reduction in cleavage was observed in comparison to the WT RNA (Fig. 5, lanes 2, 6). Thus, the ACA mutation decreased RNase E cleavage of the Spot 42 RNA at site 1, but not site 2. The addition of CsrA resulted in a modest 20–30% protection of the mutant Spot 42 RNA from RNase E cleavage at both sites (Fig. 5, lane 8). Thus, mutation of the Spot 42 GGA motif caused a modest decrease in the binding affinity of CsrA (Fig. 3B vs. 2A) and a decrease in CsrA-mediated protection from RNase E cleavage (Fig. 5). Taken together, these experiments indicate that the GGA motif is important both for efficient cleavage of Spot 42 by RNase E and for CsrA binding and protection from cleavage, thus establishing a molecular basis for Spot 42 stabilization by CsrA in vivo (Fig. 4).

To determine whether Hfq plays a role in the CsrA-mediated protection of Spot 42 from RNase E, RNase E cleavage assays were performed +/− Hfq (Fig. 6). In this experiment, CsrA alone protected Spot 42 from RNase E cleavage at both sites, resulting in a ~50 to 55% reduction in intensity of cleavage products (Fig. 6A, lane 4 and 8). Hfq protected site 1 from cleavage (Fig. 6A lane 4, 6B lane 6). CsrA-mediated protection at site 1 was observed in the presence of Hfq (Fig. 6B, lane 6 and 10). The ratio of RNase E cleavage in reactions plus or minus Hfq also showed that Hfq provided protection at site 2 (Fig. 6A lane 4, 5B lane 6), and protection by CsrA was eliminated at site 2 in the presence of Hfq (Fig. 6B lane 6 vs. 10). Thus, CsrA protection of site 1 occurs independently of Hfq. CsrA did not alter RNase E cleavage at site 2 in the presence of 3 μM Hfq (Fig. 6B, lane 6 and 10), suggesting that bound Hfq prevented CsrA-dependent protection of site 2.

sec.title: CsrA and Spot 42 Effects on srlA of the Sorbitol (srl) Locus
sec.p: Based on previous RNA-seq, CLIP-seq, and pull-down high-throughput assays, CsrA regulates 14 genes that were previously identified as Spot 42 targets (Beisel & Storz, 2011a, Beisel & Storz, 2011b, Edwards et al., 2011, Potts et al., 2017, Potts et al., 2018) (Table S2). Spot 42 negatively regulates genes for metabolism of alternative carbohydrates under catabolite repression conditions, e.g. in the presence of glucose (Beisel & Storz, 2011b). In this condition, CsrA is active and positively regulates the expression of glycolysis genes (Sabnis et al., 1995, Potts et al., 2017), suggesting that the CsrA effects on Spot 42 may increase the posttranscriptional repression of genes for metabolism of alternative carbohydrates. To determine whether CsrA-mediated stabilization of Spot 42 affects Spot 42 targets, we chose to explore the regulation of srlA. An epistasis analysis employing qRT-PCR was used to examine CsrA and Spot 42 effects on the srlA transcript. srlA mRNA levels were significantly repressed by each regulator, by at least 2-fold (Fig. 7A). Furthermore, srlA expression in the csrA::gm Δspf double mutant was synergistically increased (up to ~12-fold) relative to the WT strain (Fig. 7A). CsrA and Spot 42 regulatory effects were particularly strong during the transition to stationary phase in KB containing glucose (Fig. 7A). A similar pattern was observed in exponentially growing cultures in casamino acids-enriched M9 medium, except that the regulatory effects were highest earlier in the growth cycle (Fig. S3). These results indicate that CsrA and Spot 42 regulate the expression of the srl operon independently and can act synergistically.

sec.title: CsrA Posttranscriptionally Regulates the srlA Transcription Activator, SrlM
sec.p: Transcriptomic analyses suggested that CsrA negatively affects srlM mRNA stability (Potts et al., 2017), suggesting that CsrA might regulate srlA expression through effects on SrlM levels. To further dissect the regulation of srlA by CsrA and Spot 42, epistasis analyses of these factors on steady-state transcript levels of srlM and srlR were conducted. As shown in Figure 7B, CsrA repressed srlM RNA levels throughout growth. Spot 42 did not significantly affect srlM transcript levels. In contrast, the srlR mRNA, which encodes the srlA repressor SrlR, was not significantly affected by csrA or spf mutations (Fig. 7C). Thus, CsrA regulates srlM independently of Spot 42 under these growth conditions and does not regulate srlR. To determine if CsrA binds to srlM mRNA, a probe consisting of the srlM 5’ UTR was prepared and used to measure in vitro binding of CsrA to srlM via EMSA. Indeed, CsrA bound with high affinity to srlM RNA, forming a single complex and yielding an apparent KD value of 14 nM (Fig. 8A). These observations suggest that CsrA may directly regulate expression of srlM by binding to this mRNA at its 5’ UTR.

To determine whether CsrA effects on srlM expression are mediated directly and posttranscriptionally, coupled transcription-translation in the PURExpress system was used to measure lacZ expression from plasmid-borne srlM’-’lacZ and srlA’-’lacZ reporter fusions driven by a T7 RNA polymerase promoter. srlM’-’lacZ expression was inhibited by CsrA, while a similarly constructed control reporter fusion (pnp’-’lacZ) and the srlA’-’lacZ fusion were unaffected (Fig. 8B). Thus, CsrA directly represses translation of srlM via its 5’ UTR. The effects of Hfq and Spot 42 on srlM and srlA cell-free translation were also examined in this system (Fig. 8C). As predicted, srlA reporter expression was repressed by Spot 42 or Hfq alone and to a greater extent when added together. In contrast, srlM expression showed no such inhibition (Fig. 8C).

RNase T1 footprinting of CsrA-srlM binding was conducted to provide more detailed resolution of the CsrA:srlM binding interaction. The 5’ leader of srlM contains 4 GGA sequences (Fig. 8A). In vitro synthesized srlM transcript containing the 5’ UTR and the first 17 codons was 5’ end-labeled and tested for protection by CsrA. The srlM 5’ UTR was protected from RNase T1-mediated cleavage with increasing concentrations of CsrA (Fig. 8D). The protected nucleotides correspond to the Shine-Dalgarno (SD) sequence, which contains a GGA motif, through the start codon (Fig. 8D). Collectively, these results imply that CsrA posttranscriptionally represses srlM mRNA translation through steric inhibition of ribosome binding. As bacterial mRNA translation and stability are often coupled, the effect of CsrA on srlM translation may account for its observed effects on srlM decay (Potts et al., 2017) and mRNA levels (Fig. 7B).

sec.title: CsrA-dependent Regulation of srlA Involves effects on Spot 42 levels
sec.p: Genetic evidence suggests that Spot 42 repression of srlA occurs through direct base-pairing to the 5’ UTR at a region overlapping the srlA Shine-Dalgarno (SD) sequence (Beisel & Storz, 2011b) (Fig. S4A). Examination of the srlA 5’-UTR (nt −41 to +1 relative to A of ATG start codon) showed a GGA motif at the SD sequence, which is located within a short hairpin structure predicted by Mfold (Zuker, 2003) (Fig. S4B). In theory, this GGA motif might serve as a CsrA-binding regulatory site. To investigate the posttranscriptional regulatory effects of CsrA and Spot 42 on srlA expression, epistasis analysis was performed using a translational reporter fusion containing the 5’ UTR and 2 codons of srlA expressed from a constitutive promoter (lacUV5) (Fig. 7D). Expression of this reporter in the WT, csrA::gm, Δspf, and csrA::gm Δspf strains was tested (Fig. 7D). CsrA caused 2-fold repression, while Spot 42 showed up to 5-fold repression in this analysis. However, in the Δspf background, CsrA failed to show any effect on the reporter expression. Regulation of srlA expression showed these patterns at mid-exponential and during the transition to stationary phase growth. We conclude that Spot 42 functions epistatically in this experiment with respect to CsrA, which implies that the posttranscriptional regulatory effects of CsrA on srlA expression are mediated entirely through Spot 42. In addition, EMSA studies indicate that CsrA does not bind directly to srlA 5’ UTR mRNA (Fig. S4C). Together with findings from qRT-PCR (Fig. 7A), these results imply that posttranscriptional effects of CsrA on srlA expression are mediated via Spot 42, while CsrA effects on srlA transcription occur independently of Spot 42.

To further explore the possibility that CsrA-mediated protection of Spot 42 affects srlA expression, the Spot 42 GGA sequence motif was replaced with ACA (Fig. 9A). Additionally, point mutations to disrupt the base-pairing of Spot 42 to the srlA leader (UCU to AGA) were introduced to probe the pairing interaction between Spot 42 and srlA mRNA (Fig. 9A and S4A). WT and GGA to ACA mutant Spot 42 RNAs were expressed from a plasmid under the control of an IPTG-inducible promoter in strains containing the srlA 5’ UTR reporter, which was expressed from a constitutive promoter (lacUV5). The regulatory effects of Spot 42 in WT and csrA::gm strains growing at mid-exponential phase were analyzed. In the absence of Spot 42 induction, srlA was expressed at almost the same levels in all strains (Fig. 9B). A trend consisting of a slight decrease (~15%) in reporter expression in the csrA::gm strains vs. csrA WT strains was not statistically significant, but may suggest weak posttranscriptional activation by CsrA. This weak trend was eliminated when IPTG was added to the cultures to induce spf expression (at OD600=0.01). As compared to the pLac empty vector control, induction of WT Spot 42 (pSpfWT) repressed the expression of srlA by about 4-fold (Fig. 9C). Expression of the ACA-mutant Spot 42 (pSpfACA) also repressed the srlA leader fusion, but to a lesser extent than WT Spot 42. These data suggest that reduced CsrA binding to Spot 42 partially relieves Spot 42-dependent repression of srlA expression because decreased CsrA binding leads to increased turnover and thus lower levels of the ACA mutant Spot 42 relative to WT (Fig. 9C). The latter conclusion was supported by the finding that the levels of ACA mutant RNA were significantly lower than WT RNA levels in vivo (Fig. S5). In the csrA mutant, WT and ACA-mutant Spot 42 showed no statistically significant difference in srlA regulation (Fig. 9C), consistent with a mechanism in which posttranscriptional regulation of srlA by CsrA involves its binding to the GGA motif in Spot 42. Disruption of the srlA base-pairing region within spf (pSpf_m2) largely abolished regulation by Spot 42 in both WT and csrA mutant backgrounds (Fig. 9C), as shown previously in the WT strain (Beisel & Storz, 2011b). Lastly, EMSA analysis was used to determine if CsrA might alter the base-pairing interaction of Spot 42 with srlA mRNA. As shown in Fig. S6, pairing of these two RNAs required the presence of Hfq at concentrations of Spot 42 below 100 nM. CsrA did not promote pairing in the presence or absence of Hfq. These observations suggest that CsrA binding to Spot 42 does not alter its intrinsic base-pairing interaction with srlA mRNA. Altogether, our findings are consistent with the idea that a simple increase in Spot 42 levels accounts for all of the posttranscriptional effects of CsrA on srlA expression that are mediated via Spot 42.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: CsrA has long been known to interact with mRNAs and noncoding sRNAs of the Csr-family (reviewed in (Romeo & Babitzke, 2018, Pourciau et al., 2020). These sRNAs contain several high-affinity CsrA binding sites that contain a GGA motif in the loops of RNA structures or in single-stranded regions, which sequester CsrA away from mRNA targets. Here we show that CsrA binding to the base-pairing sRNA Spot 42 of E. coli protects this RNA from RNase E-mediated cleavage (Fig. 5) and turnover (Fig. 4) and that this leads to repression of srlA mRNA levels (Fig. 7A) and the repression of srlA translation by Spot 42 (Figs. 7D, 8B, C). CsrA binding interactions with base-pairing sRNAs have been identified previously (Potts et al., 2017, Holmqvist et al., 2016, Müller et al., 2019) and proposed to cause sequestration of CsrA (Jørgensen et al., 2013, Wachter et al., 2019). However, the CsrA:Spot 42 interaction represents the first identification of a mechanism in which CsrA binding to a base-pairing sRNA affects its stability and thus its levels, which has implications for its regulation of target mRNAs. The CsrA and Spot 42 regulons overlap (Table S2), the implications of which demand further study. Nevertheless, the growing number of CsrA-sRNA interactions that are being uncovered by transcriptomics approaches suggest that connections between these two systems have the potential to shape global gene expression networks.

Spot 42 sRNA employs three unstructured regions to pair with various mRNA targets (Beisel & Storz, 2011b, Beisel et al., 2012, Bækkedal & Haugen, 2015) (Fig. 3A). RNase E cleaves Spot 42 RNA in vitro at two closely located sites (A57, A51) within its third unstructured region (Fig. 3A, 5), one of which (A57) was identified in vivo in Salmonella (Chao et al., 2017). The sole GGA motif of Spot 42, which overlaps A57, is needed for efficient RNase E cleavage at A57 but not at A51, as shown by GGA to ACA substitution (Figs. 5, 6A). The GGA sequence is also involved in CsrA binding (Figs. 2, 3). Interestingly, CsrA protects Spot 42 from RNase E cleavage at both A57 and A51 (Fig. 5), consistent with its role in stabilizing this RNA from RNase E-dependent turnover in vivo (Fig. 4).

CsrA binding can protect mRNA (Wei et al., 2001, Yakhnin et al., 2013), Csr-family sRNA (Vakulskas et al., 2016), and based on the present studies, a base-pairing sRNA from RNase E-mediated decay (Figs. 4, 5). RNase E often catalyzes the rate-limiting step of RNA turnover via 5’ end-dependent or direct-entry pathways (Stead et al., 2011, Mackie, 2013b). CsrA protects RNA from cleavage by binding to GGA-containing motifs. Decay of flhDC mRNA proceeds via a 5’ end-dependent pathway, which requires removal of the 5’ pyrophosphate by RppH prior to RNase E cleavage (Yakhnin et al., 2013). CsrA binding to this RNA apparently prevents RppH access and RNase E cleavage. In contrast, CsrB turnover is RppH-independent. CsrA directly protects CsrB from RNase E-dependent cleavage by binding just upstream of the intrinsic terminator (Vakulskas et al., 2016). In Spot 42, CsrA binding limits RNase E access to two centrally located cleavage sites (Fig. 5, 6). We are not aware of the RNase E cleavage pathway for Spot 42, but in either model, direct steric inhibition of cleavage by CsrA would produce the same outcome.

Previous studies show that Hfq can stabilize Spot 42 from decay in vivo (Møller et al., 2002a). Our in vitro results further indicate that Hfq prevents RNase E access to both cleavage sites in the absence of CsrA and that protection of these sites by CsrA or Hfq can be mediated independently (Fig. 6). Hfq functions as an RNA chaperone, which promotes duplex formation between sRNA-mRNA pairs (Vogel & Luisi, 2011). When an Hfq-dependent sRNA base-pairs with its target mRNA, one of two alternative decay pathways can occur: 1) a stoichiometric pathway, in which the sRNA is degraded together with the mRNA molecule (Massé et al., 2003, Aiba, 2007), or 2) a recycling pathway, in which the sRNA is reused for the regulation of another mRNA molecule (Overgaard et al., 2009, Vogel, 2009). We propose that Spot 42 is degraded via stoichiometric pairing with its mRNA partners, as indicated by its rapid turnover while mRNA is being synthesized and its stabilization after mRNA has been depleted (Fig. 4 and associated Results).

Our studies indicate that CsrA effects on srlA mRNA are partly a consequence of its regulation of the srlA transcription factor srlM (Fig. 8) along with its effects on Spot 42 (Fig. 4A, B). CsrA binds tightly to srlM mRNA, directly represses srlM translation (Fig. 8A, B), and activates srlM mRNA decay in vivo (Potts et al., 2017). The latter effects on srlM mRNA are likely a consequence of translational inhibition, as the decay rate of an mRNA is often determined by the status of its translation (Deana & Belasco, 2005). Measurement of srlA mRNA levels in WT, csrA, spf, and the csrA spf double mutant strains showed that the combination of Spot 42 and CsrA regulation can mediate synergistic effects on srlA; csrA disruption caused up to ~10-fold increase in expression in the spf background (Fig. 7A, S3). In comparison, the effects of CsrA that are mediated posttranscriptionally via Spot 42 were modest, ~2 to 3-fold (Fig. 7D). Thus, we propose that the effects of CsrA on Spot 42 help to reinforce its primary regulatory role in sorbitol metabolism, which is to repress srlM translation and mRNA levels (Fig. 8B, 7B) and indirectly, transcription of srlA (Fig. 7A).

Spot 42 negatively regulates many operons involved in cellular metabolism, especially uptake and catabolism of nonpreferred carbon sources. Furthermore, spf transcription is repressed by cAMP-CRP. In the presence of glucose, decreased cAMP levels cause Spot 42 RNA to accumulate several-fold, up to 100–200 copies per cell (Sahagan & Dahlberg, 1979). CRP activates many Spot 42 target genes transcriptionally, while Spot 42 represses them posttranscriptionally via base-pairing with their 5’ UTRs at the ribosome binding site. Thus, CRP and Spot 42 act in a multioutput coherent feedforward loop (Beisel & Storz, 2011b) (Fig. 10). This circuitry reduces leaky expression of target genes under steady-state growth in the presence of glucose. Under carbon source shifts, Spot 42 accelerated the repression of alternative carbohydrate utilization genes when glucose was made available, and delayed the activation of target genes when cAMP was added to simulate glucose depletion (Beisel & Storz, 2011b). Further studies are required to understand how the negative feedforward circuity from CsrA to srlA affects expression dynamics (Fig. 10).

The expression levels of 13 Spot 42-regulated genes were repressed by CsrA in the presence of glucose (Potts et al., 2018), most of whose mRNAs did not copurify with or crosslink to CsrA in vivo (Edwards et al., 2011, Potts et al., 2017) (Table S2). Thus, CsrA-Spot 42 interactions may affect expression of other Spot 42 targets. As described in Supplemental Information, other Spot 42 mRNA targets (xylF, nanC) showed complex CsrA effects that were distinct from those of srlA (Fig. S7). A full understanding of CsrA regulation at these and other Spot 42 regulated genes will require more detailed studies.

CsrA is extensively integrated into the regulatory network of E. coli, where it directly and indirectly regulates many other regulators, perhaps several base-pairing sRNAs that bind to CsrA in vivo (Potts et al. 2017). sRNAs allow quick regulatory control of important biological processes and adjustment of sRNA stability can be a crucial way to modulate their activity (Nitzan et al., 2017). Conceivably, CsrA binding to sRNAs might interfere with sRNA-mRNA base-pairing interactions, e.g. at Spot 42 nucleation site III, which encompasses the CsrA binding site (Fig. 3A), although this remains to be demonstrated. Our findings indicate that CsrA-mediated protection of Spot 42 RNA from RNase E cleavage and stabilization in vivo reinforces the biological role of Spot 42 in carbon source selection. Because sRNAs are often induced under stresses, the full cohort of CsrA-binding sRNAs is unlikely to have been identified. We would not be surprised to find that the interactions between CsrA and Spot 42 studied here represent a common regulatory strategy for CsrA to influence sRNA function.

sec.title: Bacterial Strains, Plasmids, Bacteriophage and Culture Conditions
sec.p: All E. coli K-12 strains, plasmids, and bacteriophage used in this study are listed in Table S1. Bacterial strains were maintained in LB medium (0.5% yeast extract, 1% tryptone, and 1% NaCl, pH 7.4) containing the following antibiotics when appropriate: ampicillin (100 μg ml−1), tetracycline (15 μg ml−1), kanamycin (100 μg ml−1), gentamicin (10 μg ml−1), and chloramphenicol (25 μg ml−1). Overnight cultures were routinely inoculated in LB medium (2 ml) with bacterial strains from frozen (−80°C) glycerol stocks and were grown at 37°C or 30°C with shaking (250 rpm). Kornberg (KB) medium (1.1% K2HPO4, 0.85% KH2PO4, 0.6% yeast extract containing 0.5% glucose or 0.25% glycerol) was used for reporter gene expression analyses and Northern blot analyses. Casamino acids-enriched M9 medium (1X M9 salts supplemented with 10 μg /ml thiamine, 2 mM MgSO4, 0.1 mM CaCl2, 0.2% casamino acids containing 0.2% glucose) (Beisel & Storz, 2011b) was used as indicated. Gene deletions were introduced by the standard P1vir transduction procedure from E. coli donor strains of the Keio library (Baba et al., 2006) or the λ red recombinase method as previously described (Datsenko & Wanner, 2000). The Flp recombinase encoded in pCP20 (Cherepanov & Wackernagel, 1995) was used to eliminate antibiotic resistance cassettes, as required. pBRplac plasmid was obtained from Dr. Susan Gottesman (Guillier & Gottesman, 2006) and pSpot42 was constructed as described previously (Beisel & Storz, 2011b). All oligonucleotide primers (listed in Table S1) used in this study were synthesized by Integrated DNA Technologies, Coralville, IA.

sec.title: Construction of lacZ Reporter Fusions
sec.p: Posttranscriptional fusions to lacZ were constructed in plasmid placUV5 as the vector using the CRIM system (Haldimann & Wanner, 2001), as described previously (Edwards et al., 2011). The posttranscriptional lacZ reporter fusions containing the 5’ untranslated regions of srlA (nt −41 to +5 relative to the A residue of the ATG start codon) were constructed by annealing the oligonucleotide pairs “srlA lacUV5 Fw/ srlA lacUV5 comp” to generate DNA with EcoRI- and BamHI-compatible 5’ overhangs. The resulting double-stranded DNA was ligated into EcoRI- and BamHI-digested placUV5 and transformed into electrocompetent DH5α λpir cells. The resulting plasmid was called pUV5srlA’-’lacZ and was sequenced with primers LPF-12 and LPF-13 to verify the absence of mutations within the 5’ UTR of srlA. pUV5srlA’-’lacZ was integrated into the λatt site of strain MG1655 ΔlacZ carrying the helper plasmid pFINT. Single integrants were confirmed by PCR as described previously (Haldimann & Wanner, 2001).

sec.title: β-galactosidase Assay
sec.p: Bacterial cultures containing translational fusions with lacZ were grown in LB at 37°C to exponential phase, diluted to an optical density at 600 nm (OD600) of 0.01 in fresh KB medium. Cells were harvested at various time points throughout growth. β-galactosidase activity was determined as described previously (Edwards et al., 2011). Total cellular protein was measured following precipitation with 10% trichloroacetic acid (v/v), using the bicinchoninic acid (BCA) assay (Pierce Biotechnology). Bovine serum albumin served as the protein standard.

sec.title: RNA Preparation and Decay Analysis
sec.p: E. coli strains were grown in LB at 37°C or at 30°C to exponential phase, diluted to an OD600 of 0.01 in fresh KB and then grown to mid exponential phase (OD600 ~0.4). Rifampicin was added to a final concentration of 200 μg/ml to stop transcription initiation. Samples were harvested at 0, 1, 2, 4, 8, and 16 min following rifampicin addition and immediately mixed with ice-cold 1/8 sample volume of stop solution (10% phenol: 90% ethanol [v/v]). Total cellular RNA was isolated using hot phenol-chloroform extraction followed by ethanol precipitation. Genomic DNA was digested by treating 20 μg of nucleic acids with 2 U of Turbo DNase (Roche), and RNA was purified from these reactions with phenol-chloroform extraction followed by ethanol precipitation. Spot 42 or rpsT RNA levels were detected by Northern blotting and normalized to the 5S rRNA levels. Semilog decay curves were determined using Prism (GraphPad).

sec.title: Northern Blotting
sec.p: Northern blot analysis was performed as previously described (Leng et al., 2016) with minor modifications. Total cellular RNA (1–2 μg) was mixed with equal volume of 2X loading buffer (95% formamide, 0.025% SDS, 0.025% bromophenol blue, 0.025% xylene cyanol, 20 mM EDTA), denatured by heating at 85°C for 5 min, placed on ice, and separated on denaturing 7M urea- 6% polyacrylamide gels in 1X Tris-borate-EDTA (TBE) buffer (89 mM tris base, 89 mM boric acid and 2 mM EDTA) at a constant voltage at 150 V at room temperature. RNA was transferred by electroblotting at a constant current of 150 mA in the cold room for 8 h and fixed to a positively charged nylon membrane (Roche) by exposure to UV light (120,000 μJ) and hybridized overnight at 65°C with digoxigenin (DIG)-labeled antisense RNA probes as previously described (Pannuri et al., 2016). Transcripts were detected using the DIG Northern Starter kit (Roche Diagnostics) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Blots were imaged using the ChemiDoc XRS+ system (Bio-Rad), and signal intensity was quantified using Quantity One image analysis software (Bio-Rad). 5S rRNA levels served as loading controls for signal correction.

sec.title: Quantitative Reverse Transcriptase PCR (qRT-PCR)
sec.p: Quantitative reverse transcriptase PCR (qRT-PCR) was conducted using the iTaq Universal SYBR green one-step kit (Bio-Rad) and an iCycler iQ5 real-time PCR detection system (Bio-Rad) according to the manufacturer’s instructions as described previously (Renda et al., 2020). Total cellular RNA was isolated using hot phenol-chloroform extraction followed by ethanol precipitation. Genomic DNA was digested by treating 20 μg of nucleic acids with 2 U of Turbo DNase (Roche), and RNA was purified from these reactions with phenol-chloroform extraction followed by ethanol precipitation. The overall integrity of the RNA was assessed by observing rRNA following 2% denaturing formaldehyde agarose gel electrophoresis in the presence of ethidium bromide and imaged using a ChemiDoc XRS system (Bio-Rad). The primer sequences used for qRT-PCR analysis were listed in Table S1. Reaction mixtures (10 μl) contained 200 ng of RNA or standard DNA, 300 nM each primer, iScript reverse transcriptase, and 1x iTaq Universal SYBR green reaction mix. Reaction mixtures were incubated for 10 min of reverse transcription (RT) at 50°C, 1 min of RT inactivation at 90°C, followed by 45 cycles of denaturation for 10 s at 95°C and annealing/extension for 20 s at 60°C. Following amplification, melting curve analysis was used to verify the specificity of the PCR product according to a single melting temperature (Tm). Melting curve analysis consisted of incubation for 1 min at 95°C, 1 min at 60°C, followed by 70 steps in which the temperature was increased to 95°C at a rate of 0.5°C /10 s/step. The target mRNA quantities were determined relative to a DNA standard curve for the PCR products using iQ5 optical system software version 2.1 (Bio-Rad) and were normalized to 16S rRNA levels.

sec.title: Purification of CsrAHis6
sec.p: Recombinant CsrAHis6 was purified as previously described (Vakulskas et al., 2016) with minor modifications. 1 liter of E. coli Tuner (DE3) cells carrying pCSB12 (Dubey et al., 2005) were grown at 30°C in LB containing ampicillin to an OD600 of 0.6. Then, 1 mM isopropyl-β-D-thiogalactopyranoside (IPTG) was added to the culture, which was incubated for an additional 6 h incubation at 30°C. Bacteria were harvested by centrifugation (6,000 × g, 10 min, 4°C) and resuspended in 20 ml lysis buffer (50 mM Tris pH 8.0, 300 mM NaCl, 20 mM imidazole and 2 tablets of protease inhibitor cocktail (Roche). Cells were disrupted on ice using a pre-chilled French pressure cell (1,500 psi), and the CsrAHis6-containing lysates were cleared by centrifugation twice (15,000 × g, 15 min, 4°C) and then subjected to a 1mL Hi-Trap Ni2+ Column (GE Healthcare) previously equilibrated in lysis buffer. The column was washed with 20 ml of wash buffer A (50 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0, 1 M NaCl and 20 mM imidazole) followed by 20 ml of wash buffer B (50 mM Tris pH 8.0, 300 mM NaCl and 50 mM imidazole). CsrAHis6 was eluted with 0–100% gradient over 40 ml of wash buffer B containing 50 mM to 250 mM imidazole. Peak protein fractions were pooled and dialyzed against CsrA storage buffer (10 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0, 100 mM KCl, 10 mM MgCl2 and 25% glycerol). Aliquots of purified CsrAHis6 were flash frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored at −80°C. Protein concentrations were determined using the BCA assay (Pierce Biotechnology) with bovine serum albumin as the protein standard.

sec.title: Purification of Hfq
sec.p: Untagged Hfq was purified as previously described (Santiago-Frangos et al., 2016) with minor modifications. Plasmid pET21b-Hfq was obtained from Dr. Sarah Woodson (Santiago-Frangos et al., 2016) and expressed in E. coli BL21 (DE3) cells in 1-liter LB containing ampicillin at 37°C to an OD600 of 0.6. One mM IPTG was added to the culture, which was incubated for an additional 3 h incubation at 37°C. Cells were precipitated by centrifugation (6,000 × g, 10 min, 4°C) and resuspended in 50 ml lysis buffer (50 mM Tris-HCl, pH8.0, 250 mM MgCl2, 1.5 M NaCl and 1 mM 2-mercaptoethanol) and lysed by sonication (Sonic Dismembrator Model 500, Fisher Scientific) on ice at 25% amplitude over 10 min (a 5 s sonication followed by a 5 s pause). The lysate was clarified by centrifugation (27,000 × g at 4°C for 20 min) and treated with 50 U/ml benzonase nuclease (Sigma-Aldrich) at room temperature for 15 min. The lysate was heated to 80°C for 15 min to inactivate benzonase and clarified by centrifugation (27,000 × g at 4°C for 20 min) and filtration through a 0.45-μm filter (Millipore). The clarified lysate was applied to a 1 mL Hi-Trap Ni2+ Column (GE Healthcare) previously equilibrated in lysis buffer. The column was washed with wash buffer (50 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0, 1.5 M NaCl, 25 mM imidazole and 0.5 mM 2-mercaptoethanol). Hfq was eluted with 0–100% gradient over 40 ml of the same buffers containing 25 mM to 250 mM imidazole. Desired fractions were pooled and dialyzed into Hfq storage buffer (50 mM Tris pH 7.5, 1 mM EDTA, 250 mM NH4Cl, 10% glycerol). Aliquots of purified Hfq were flash frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored at −80°C. Protein concentrations were determined using the BCA assay (Pierce Biotechnology) with bovine serum albumin as the protein standard. The concentration of Hfq herein refers to the hexamer.

sec.title: Electrophoretic Gel Mobility Shift Assay (EMSA) for RNA Binding
sec.p: The binding of CsrA and/or Hfq to Spot 42, srlA and srlM transcripts was determined by EMSA using in vitro-synthesized Spot 42, srlA and srlM transcripts and recombinant CsrAHis6 and Hfq. The template DNAs for in vitro transcription were generated by PCR from MG1655 genomic DNA using oligonucleotide pairs listed in Table S1. The template DNA for in vitro transcription of srlA was generated by annealing the oligonucleotide pair srlA T7 EMSA/ srlA T7 EMSA comp. RNA was synthesized from the Spot 42 (109 nt, full length of spf), srlA (44 nt, consisting of the 31 nt of the 5’ UTR and 3 nt of the coding region) and srlM templates (107 nt, consisting of 104 nt of the 5’ UTR and 3 nt of the coding region) in vitro using the MEGAshortscript kit (Ambion). The resulting transcripts were gel purified followed by phenol-chloroform extraction and ethanol precipitation. Transcripts were treated with Antarctic phosphatase (NEB) and radiolabeled at the 5’ end using [γ−32P] ATP (Perkin Elmer) and T4 polynucleotide kinase (NEB). Radiolabeled RNA was gel purified and resuspended in TE (10 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0, 1 mM EDTA).

Prior to EMSA, RNA samples were heated to 90°C for 3 min and chilled on ice. Binding reaction mixtures contained 0.5 nM radiolabeled RNA, 10 mM MgCl2, 100 mM KCl, 32.5 ng total yeast RNA, 20 mM dithiothreitol (DTT), 7.5% glycerol, 4 U SUPERasin (Ambion), and various concentrations of recombinant CsrAHis6 and/or Hfq and were incubated at 37°C for 30 min. Reaction mixtures were separated on 9% native polyacrylamide gels (for Spot 42 and srlM mRNA) or 12% native polyacrylamide gels (for srlA mRNA) with 1X TBE buffer. Competition assays were performed in the presence or absence of unlabeled specific (self) and nonspecific (phoB) RNA competitors using the minimum CsrA concentration required for a full shift. Labeled RNA was analyzed using a phosphorimager equipped with Quantity One software (Bio-Rad), as previously described (Edwards et al., 2011). The apparent equilibrium binding constant (KD) for CsrA-RNA or Hfq-RNA complex formation was calculated according to a previously described cooperative binding equation (Mercante et al., 2006).

sec.title: Generation of Base-Substituted Spot 42
sec.p: The Spot 42 plasmid, pSpot42, was generated by PCR amplification of the spf gene using primer pair spf AatII Fw/ spf EcoRI Rv (Table S1). Purified DNA was digested with AatII and EcoRI and cloned into the corresponding restriction sites of pBRplac. The resulting plasmid, pSpot42, was used as template to introduce base changes using the QuikChange II XL kit (Agilent Technologies) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. pSpot42_ACA was generated by using primer pair spf ACA Fw/ spf ACA comp. pSpot42_II was generated by using primer pair spf II Fw/ spf II comp. The mutations were confirmed by sequencing.

sec.title: Coupled Transcription-Translation Assay
sec.p: In vitro coupled transcription-translation assays using PURExpress (NEB) followed a published procedure (Park et al., 2015). Plasmid pEL1 contains a T7 promoter driving transcription of the srlA translational fusion (nt +1 to +96 relative to the srlA transcriptional start site). Plasmid pEL2 contains a T7 promoter driving transcription of the srlM translational fusion (nt −41 to +53 relative to the srlM translation ATG start codon). These plasmids were used as the templates for coupled transcription-translation reactions using the PURExpress in vitro protein synthesis kit, according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Each 6.7-μl reaction mixture contained 150 ng of plasmid DNA template, various concentrations of purified CsrAHis6, Hfq hexamer, Spot 42 RNA, 1 U of RNasin (Promega), 2.5 mM DTT, 2.7 μl of solution A, and 2 μl of solution B. The mixtures were incubated at 37°C for 2 h, and β-galactosidase activity was determined according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

sec.title: In Vitro RNase E Cleavage Assay
sec.p: Purification of RNase E was described in previous study (Vakulskas et al., 2016). Spot 42 RNA was dephosphorylated with Quick CIP (NEB) and radiolabeled at the 5’ end using T4 polynucleotide kinase (NEB) and [γ−32P] ATP (7,000 Ci/mmol) (Perkin Elmer). Labeled RNAs were denatured by heating at 90°C for 1 min followed by slow cooling to room temperature. Reaction mixtures (10 μl) contained 0.6 nM labeled RNA, 25 mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.9, 5 mM MgCl2, 60 mM KCl, 7.5% glycerol (v/v), 15 mM DTT, 100 mM NH4Cl, 2 μg acetylated BSA (Promega) and 0 or 3 μM CsrAHis6. After a 30-min incubation at 37°C, 15 nM RNase E was added, and incubation was continued at 37°C for 60 min. The reactions were stopped by adding 10 μl of stop solution (95% formamide, 0.025% SDS, 20 mM EDTA, 0.025% bromophenol blue, 0.025% xylene cyanol). Samples were heated at 95°C for 5 min and fractionated through 6% polyacrylamide-8 M urea sequencing gels. Cleavage patterns were examined using a phosphorimager. For quantitation of CsrA or Hfq effects on cleavage, the less changed bands in each lane served as loading controls. The background signal was calculated from the Intensity of the same area in the lane without RNase treatment. Signal intensities of S1 and S2 cleavages were determined by subtracting the background intensity, normalizing to the loading control, and then comparing to the intensity of the reaction with RNase E.

sec.title: Footprint Assay
sec.p: CsrA-srlM RNA footprint assays followed a published procedure (Yakhnin et al., 2013). srlM RNA (from nt − 41 to + 53 relative to the start of srlM translation) was synthesized with the RNAMaxx kit (Agilent Technologies) using pEL2 plasmid digested with BamHI as DNA template. srlA RNA (from + 1 to + 96 relative to the start of srlA transcription) was synthesized with the RNAMaxx kit (Agilent Technologies) using pEL1 plasmid digested with BamHI as DNA template. A PCR fragment containing from +1 to +109 of spf (pBRlacspf plasmid as template) was purified using the Qiagen PCR purification kit and Spot 42 RNA was synthesized with the RNAMaxx kit (Agilent Technologies). Gel-purified RNAs were dephosphorylated with Quick CIP (NEB) and then 5′ end labeled using T4 polynucleotide kinase (NEB) and [γ−32P] ATP (7,000 Ci/mmol) (Perkin Elmer). Labeled RNAs were denatured by heating at 90°C for 1 min followed by slow cooling to room temperature. Binding reaction mixtures (10 μl) contained 5 nM labeled RNA, 10 mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.5, 10 mM MgCl2, 100 mM KCl, 40 ng of yeast RNA, 7.5% glycerol and various concentrations of purified CsrAHis6, Hfq, Spot 42 RNA. After a 30-min incubation at 37°C, RNase T1 (0.016 U) was added, and incubation was continued for 15 min at 37°C. Samples were extracted with an equal volume of phenol and chloroform, spun down at maximum speed for 5 min, and 5 μl of the aqueous phase was mixed with 5 μl of stop solution (95% formamide, 0.025% SDS, 20 mM EDTA, 0.025% bromophenol blue, 0.025% xylene cyanol). Samples were heated at 95°C for 5 min and fractionated through standard 6% polyacrylamide-8 M urea sequencing gels. Cleaved patterns were examined using a phosphorimager.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: This project was funded by NIH R01 grant GM059969 to Tony Romeo and Paul Babitzke. We thank Sarah Woodson for providing Hfq-expressing plasmid, pET21b-Hfq, Susan Gottesman for pBRplac and Gisela Storz for xylF and nanC reporter strains.

caption.title: Sorbitol locus, uptake and metabolism.
caption.p: This locus encodes the specific PTS components for D-sorbitol uptake and utilization: EIIASrl called SrlB (pink box), EIIBCSrl is a fusion protein carrying the IIB domain and a duplication of the IIC domain, also called SrlE (light yellow box). EIICSrl also called SrlA (brown box), sorbitol dehydrogenase SrlD (light green box), the transcriptional activator SrlM (black box) and the transcriptional repressor SrlR (gray box). cAMP-CRP is shown in purple. Activation of gene expression is indicated by broken arrows; repression is shown by a broken T bar. This figure was generated in Biorender.com.

caption.title: In vitro binding assays of Spot 42 RNA with CsrA and/or Hfq.
caption.p: (A) Electrophoretic mobility shift analysis (EMSA) of CsrA binding to Spot 42 transcripts. 5′-end-labeled transcripts (0.5 nM) were incubated with CsrA at the concentrations shown. (B) Competition reactions were performed in the presence of specific (Spot 42) or nonspecific (phoB) unlabeled competitor RNA at the concentrations shown. (C) Gel shift analyses of hexameric Hfq binding to Spot 42 transcripts. 5′-end-labeled transcripts (0.5 nM) were incubated with Hfq at the concentrations shown. The binding curve for Hfq interaction with Spot 42 is also shown. (D) EMSA of CsrA and Hfq incubated at the concentrations shown with 5′-end-labeled Spot 42 RNA. The positions of free and bound complexes are indicated.

caption.title: Spot 42 secondary structure and in vitro binding of CsrA with the GGA to ACA mutant Spot 42 RNA.
caption.p: (A) Secondary structure of Spot 42 RNA. Regions involved in Spot 42 base-pairing with mRNA targets are highlighted in yellow. Region I: nt 1–10; region II: nt 20–37; region III: nt 48–60. The sole GGA motif is circled in cyan. CsrA CLIP-seq reads determined from Potts et al., 2017 are shown by the green dashed line. Putative Hfq binding sites are circled in gray and were predicted using the consensus Hfq binding motif identified in Salmonella (Holmqvist et al., 2016). (B) EMSA of CsrA binding to the GGA to ACA mutant Spot 42 transcript. 5′-end-labeled transcripts (0.5 nM) were incubated with CsrA at the concentrations shown. The binding curves for CsrA interaction with WT and the ACA-mutant Spot 42 are shown for comparison.

caption.title: Analysis of Spot 42 RNA decay.
caption.p: (A) and (B) Decay curves of Spot 42 RNA were determined by Northern blotting of RNA from exponential phase cultures (OD600 ~0.4) at various times following the addition of rifampicin. Culture identities: E. coli MG1655 (WT) or its csrA::gm mutant. The RNA half-lives were determined from the first linear portions of their decay curves (blue lines=WT, red=csrA::gm). Error bars represented SEM of values from two independent experiments (n=4). Data from a representative experiment is shown. Steady-state RNA levels are shown in the inset. (C) Decay curves of Spot 42 RNA determined by cells expressing a single-copy of PBADspf from the chromosomal locus in WT or csrA::gm mutant. Cells were grown in LB to an OD600 of 0.4, and arabinose added (0.01%) for 15 min. The culture was centrifuged briefly (5 min) to wash out the arabinose and resuspended in an equal volume of LB with glucose (0.2%). RNAs were extracted from the samples removed at the intervals shown. One of the representative results from two biological replicates (n=2) is shown. (D) Analysis of Spot 42 RNA decay under RNase E non-permissive temperature. Decay rates of Spot 42 RNA were determined by Northern blotting of RNA extracted from WT, csrA::gm, rne-1, and rne-1 csrA::gm mutant strains, following temperature shift of cultures at mid-exponential phase of growth (OD600 ~0.25) from 30°C to 40°C and followed immediately by rifampicin addition. The RNA half-lives were determined from the linear portions of their decay curves. Error bars represented SEM of values from two independent experiments (n=4) (Some error bars were smaller than the symbol size.)

caption.title: In vitro RNase E cleavage of WT and ACA mutant Spot 42 RNA +/−CsrA.
caption.p: RNase E cleavage experiments were performed with 5’-end labeled 109 nt WT or ACA-mutant Spot 42 RNA, 3 μM CsrA and 15 nM RNase E as indicated. Positions of RNase E cleavage site 1 (red S1) and site 2 (blue S2) are marked by arrows. Site S1 is within the GGA motif of the CsrA binding site. Control experiments with RNA only are shown in lanes 1 and 5. Partial alkaline hydrolysis (OH) and RNase T1 digestion (T1) ladders are shown. Numbering is with respect to the full-length Spot 42 sequence. Intensity of RNase E cleavage for each band from three independent experiments was calculated and summarized below.

caption.title: Hfq and CsrA-mediated protection of Spot 42 from RNase E cleavage in vitro.
caption.p: RNase E cleavage experiments were performed with 5’ end-labeled WT Spot 42 RNA, 3 μM CsrA, 20 nM RNase E, in the absence (A) or presence of (B) 3 μM Hfq, as indicated. The positions of RNase E cleavage sites 1 (red arrow) and 2 (blue arrow) are marked. Control experiments with RNA only are shown at lane 1 in panel A or indicated as lane C in panel B. Partial alkaline hydrolysis (OH) and RNase T1 digestion (T1) ladders are shown. All RNA samples were analyzed on the same gel and exposed for same length of time, but separated digitally for clarity. Numbering is with respect to the full-length Spot 42 sequence.

caption.title: CsrA and Spot 42 effects on sorbitol genes.
caption.p: (A) Steady-state levels of srlA mRNA were determined by qRT-PCR of RNA extracted from WT, csrA::gm, Δspf and Δspf csrA::gm mutant strains during mid-exponential (OD600 ~0.4) and the transition to stationary phases (OD600 ~2.0) of growth in KB containing 0.5% glucose and normalized to 16S rRNA levels. (B) Steady-state levels of srlM in casamino-acid enriched M9 medium supplemented with 0.2% glucose. (C) Steady-state levels of srlR in casamino-acid enriched M9 medium supplemented with 0.2% glucose. Error bars represent SEM of values from two independent experiments with two biological replicates and two technical replicates in each experimental set (n=8). Statistical significance was determined using two-way ANOVA and denoted as follows: ***, P <0.001; **, P <0.005; *, P <0.05. (D) Construction of PlacUV5-srlA’-’lacZ leader fusion containing the 5’ UTR of srlA (nt −41 to +5 relative to the A residue of the ATG start codon) in-frame with ‘lacZ. Results of β-galactosidase assays of strains containing the PlacUV5-srlA’-’lacZ leader fusion at the chromosomal locus are shown. Effects of csrA and spf single and double mutations on expression of PlacUV5-srlA’-’lacZ. β-galactosidase activities were determined in exponential-phase (OD600 of 0.4) and transition to stationary-phase (OD600 of 2.0) cultures grown in KB with 0.5% glucose. Bars depict the mean and SEM from two independent experiments with at least two biological replicates and two technical replicates in each experiment (n=8). Statistical significance was determined using two-way ANOVA and denoted as follows: ***, P <0.001; **, P <0.005; *, P <0.05; ns, not significant, P <0.05. Note that unlike srlA mRNA levels that were highest in the csrA::gm Δspf double mutant strains (A), regulation of PlacUV5-srlA’-’lacZ by CsrA was eliminated in the spf background (D).

caption.title: CsrA-srlM RNA binding and regulation of srlA and srlM translation by CsrA, Hfq and/or Spot 42.
caption.p: (A) Sequence of EMSA probe with GGA sequences that are potential CsrA binding sites underlined. The A of the translation initiation codon (AUG) is shown in blue, and the Shine-Dalgarno (SD) sequence is underlined in yellow. 5’-end-labeled transcripts (0.5 nM) were incubated with CsrA at the concentrations shown. The binding curve for CsrA-srlM RNA interaction is shown. (B) Effects of CsrA on translation of srlM’-’lacZ or srlA’-’lacZ fusions. Relative β-galactosidase activity depicts the mean and SEM of activity relative to reaction mixtures lacking CsrA (n=6). (C) Effects of Spot 42 +/− Hfq on the 5’ UTR of srlM or srlA. Relative β-galactosidase activity depicts the mean and SEM of activity relative to reaction mixtures without Hfq and Spot 42 (n=6). Statistical significance was determined using two-way ANOVA and denoted as follows: ****, P<0.0001; ***, P <0.001; **, P <0.005; *, P <0.05. (D) CsrA-srlM RNA footprint. 5’-end-labeled srlM RNA was treated with RNase T1 +/− CsrA, as shown. Partial alkaline hydrolysis (OH) and RNase T1 digestion (T1) ladders, as well as a control lane without treatment (C), are shown. Residues protected from RNase T1 cleavage by CsrA are indicate in red.

caption.title: Effects of CsrA via wild type or mutant Spot 42 on srlA expression.
caption.p: (A) Secondary structure of Spot 42 and the regions involved in Spot 42 base-pairing with mRNA targets are highlighted in yellow. Region I: nt 1–10; region II: nt 20–37; region III: nt 48–60. GGA motif is circled in cyan. The point mutations corresponding to each site are boxed. (B) Results of β-galactosidase assay of strains containing a PlacUV5-srlA’-’lacZ leader fusion without IPTG-induction of Spot 42 synthesis or (C) with IPTG-induction of Spot 42 from plasmids. Error bars represent SEM of values from two independent experiments with three biological replicates and two technical replicates in each experimental set (n=12). Statistical significance was determined using two-way ANOVA and denoted as follows: ****, P<0.0001; ns, not significant, P <0.05.

caption.title: Model of CsrA-dependent regulation of srlA expression.
caption.p: CRP and Spot 42 participate in a coherent feedforward loop. cAMP-CRP directly activates srlA transcription and by repressing Spot 42, it indirectly activates srlA expression (Beisel & Storz, 2011b). CsrA protects Spot 42 from RNase E cleavage, thus enhancing the negative influence of Spot 42 on srlA expression. CsrA also posttranscriptionally represses srlM, which encodes for the transcription activator of the sorbitol operon. Therefore, CsrA indirectly represses srlA transcription. Activation of gene expression is indicated by arrows; repression is shown by T bars. This figure was generated in Biorender.com.
